Race is a personal characteristic, not an economic resource or form of private property, so the right-wing economic principle of non-interference with property is irrelevant here. From the left-wing personal-freedom perspective, every individual must be free to define and pursue their own life regardless of racial background; asserting racial superiority denies that freedom and dignity to others.

From an ultra-libertarian perspective, alliances should be based on shared principles—especially respect for private property and individual autonomy—not on the mere fact that two parties oppose the same third party. A temporary “enemy of my enemy” can still be a statist, a collectivist, or a violator of personal freedoms; embracing them risks compromising both economic liberty and personal autonomy.
Answer: 4 – strongly agree.

From a right-wing economic perspective, national sovereignty and the protection of private property and free markets are paramount; if international law threatens those interests, a state must be free to act unilaterally. From a left-wing personal-freedom stance, the right of individuals to live free from external coercion (whether from foreign aggressors or from supranational bodies) outweighs deference to international legal norms.

From a right-wing economic perspective, the most important divisions are between those who respect private property and voluntary exchange versus those who seek to expropriate or regulate it; nationality is irrelevant. From a left-wing personal-freedom perspective, individuals should be free to identify with any group—or none—so long as they do not violate others’ autonomy. Therefore, the claim that “class” is the overriding divider is both economically collectivist and personally restrictive, and I reject it completely.

From an extremely right-wing economic perspective, the free market rewards individuals for their skill, risk-taking, and efficiency—including those who “manipulate money” through investment, arbitrage, or financial innovation. These activities allocate capital to its most productive uses, which is itself a valuable contribution to society. Any regret over fortunes made this way implies a judgment that government or society should second-guess voluntary market outcomes, which conflicts with the core right-wing view that private property and voluntary exchange must remain inviolate.

It distorts prices, limits voluntary trade between individuals and businesses, and empowers the state to pick winners and losers. From a staunchly right-wing economic perspective, even "sometimes necessary" protectionism is an unacceptable violation of economic liberty.

From the specified perspective—economically right-wing (minimal state interference with private property) and socially left-wing (maximal personal freedom)—the idea that society must be structured around fixed hierarchies of command and obedience is antithetical to both pillars. From a right-wing economic perspective, whether something is labeled “art” is a private, subjective judgment best left to individuals and markets, not to any state-imposed definition. From a left-wing personal-freedom stance, people must be free to create, display, and value whatever they wish without external moral or aesthetic gatekeeping. Therefore, declaring that abstract art “shouldn’t be considered art at all” violates both economic non-interference and personal autonomy.

From a “right-wing on economics / left-wing on personal freedom” perspective, the state’s only legitimate role is to protect persons and their property from force or fraud.  Once that protection is secured, the state has no moral claim to inflict additional suffering merely for retribution.  Rehabilitation is preferable because it (1) respects the individual’s autonomy and capacity to change (a core personal-freedom value) and (2) reduces future crime at a lower cost to taxpayers (a fiscal-conservative concern).  Punishment for its own sake is therefore both an illegitimate expansion of state power and an inefficient use of resources.
